### 13.4 Research Digest

Per D1.10 §11.3. Batches Moderate Signal research findings from the X research lane. Sent weekly alongside the weekly summary, or on a separate declared cadence if research volume justifies it.

### 13.5 Digest Timing

All digest types have a declared send time registered in Mission Control's cron register. The human sets these times during Phase 1 setup. Default cadence:

| Digest | Default Cadence | Default Time |
|---|---|---|
| Daily digest | Daily | End of operating day (TBD) |
| Weekly summary | Weekly | End of week (TBD) |
| Research digest | Weekly | Same time as weekly summary |

Timing is adjusted by updating the cron registration — not by modifying this document.

---

## 14. Noise / Clutter Control

Clutter is the primary enemy of a useful communication layer. The rules below are enforced at the Broker level (D1.9) and define what is never sent regardless of what agents produce.

### 14.1 Messages That Are Never Sent

The following are never sent to any human-facing channel under any circumstances:

- Heartbeat confirmations ("Mac Mini is online," "gateway is running")
- In-progress status messages for tasks that have not declared a progress interval
- Raw model outputs or reasoning traces
- Internal task packet contents
- Stack traces or raw error objects
- Every task state transition (only `completed` and `escalation` generate messages)
- Duplicate notices for the same event within a single digest period
- Empty digests (suppressed)

### 14.2 Volume Cap

Telegram receives a maximum of N individual real-time messages per hour during normal operation (N is calibrated during Phase 1 and recorded in Mission Control's automation register). If more would be generated, non-critical messages are held for the next digest. Escalations and critical alerts always break the cap.

### 14.3 Drill-Down, Don't Push Down

Every Telegram message should be actionable or informational at a glance. Technical detail, full task output, and supporting evidence live in Mission Control or the proof artifact. The message contains a reference to the detail — not the detail itself. The human drills in when they want to, not because the message forced them to read everything to understand it.

### 14.4 Calm Language Standard

Messages from the swarm use plain, direct language:
- No urgency theater ("CRITICAL ALERT: something you should know...")
- No excessive emoji or visual noise
- No corporate filler ("I hope this finds you well...")
- No hedging on what happened ("it seems like the task may have completed...")
- Statements, not speculation

If something went wrong, say what went wrong. If something was completed, say what was completed. If a decision is needed, say what the decision is.

---

## 15. Blocked / TBD Communication Decisions

| Item | Blocked On | Expected Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Telegram bot token | D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md not yet written | D1.14 authored; token registered |
| Telegram chat ID and channel structure | Bot not yet configured | Phase 1 setup; recorded in D1.14 |
| Daily digest send time | Human preference not calibrated | Set during Phase 1 setup; recorded in cron register |
| Weekly summary day and time | Human preference | Set during Phase 1 setup |
| Per-hour message volume cap (N) | Requires Phase 1 operation to calibrate | Set after first week of operation |
| Sprint cadence and planning rhythm | Not yet defined | Phase 2 planning checkpoint |
| Whether Discord is used at all at Phase 1 | Human decision pending | Decide at Phase 1 setup: configure or defer |
| Escalation re-send interval calibration | Requires first live escalation for feedback | Adjusted after first escalation test |
| Research digest cadence | D1.10 §4 Mode 1 needs to be practiced first | Set after 2–4 weeks of research intake operation |
| Multi-channel escalation path (if Telegram unavailable) | D1.9 degraded mode governs; Discord may serve as explicitly approved emergency secondary path only if configured for that purpose and documented in D1.9 and D1.14 — not an automatic fallback | Confirmed when Discord decision is made and documented in D1.9 and D1.14 |

---

## 16. Immediate Communication Priorities

### Phase 1 — before agents are fully operational

1. **Write D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md and register Telegram bot token** — No coordination channel works without credentials. D1.14 must be written before any bot is configured. (Source: §6.3)

2. **Configure Telegram bot and test directive → acknowledgement → completion cycle** — The primary coordination channel must be verified working before Phase 1 is considered operational. Test: human sends directive via Telegram; bridge acknowledges within 30 seconds; task completes; completion notice appears in Telegram with correct format. (Source: D1.9 §10.1, §8.1)

3. **Decide whether to configure Discord at Phase 1** — Make an explicit decision: use Discord for a specific justified purpose, or defer it entirely. Defaulting to "we'll set it up eventually" creates deferred overhead. (Source: §7)

4. **Set daily digest time and weekly summary day** — Register both in Mission Control's cron definition file before the first digest is expected. The human should not have to think about when briefings arrive — they should just appear. (Source: §13.5, D1.8 §9.1)

5. **Set the per-hour message volume cap after the first week** — Do not set it before operating. Set it based on what the first week of Phase 1 communication actually produces. Record it in the Mission Control automation register. (Source: §14.2)

### Phase 2 — after cluster is online and first sprint begins

6. **Define sprint cadence** — Set the sprint length, planning rhythm, and how sprint goals are communicated in Mission Control and surfaced in the weekly summary. This does not need to happen before Phase 1. (Source: §10.3)

7. **Test weekly summary format with real data** — The first weekly summary is generated from Phase 1 operation. Review it with the human. Adjust the format if it is too dense, too sparse, or missing something useful. (Source: §8.5)

8. **Define Telegram structure for Phase 2 volume** — After Phase 1, assess whether the single Telegram channel is sufficient or whether a second escalations-only channel is warranted. Make this decision with Phase 1 data, not speculation. (Source: §6.3)

---

_This document is the authoritative communication policy. Broker implementation (D1.9) must be consistent with it. When channels are added or communication patterns change, this document is updated before implementation. The channel hierarchy in §4 is the default; deviations require an update here before they are activated._

docs.title: D1.12 — CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY
docs.content:
# D1.12 — CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY
**Autonomous AI Organization — How New Capabilities Are Evaluated, Approved, Registered, and Governed**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how the organization acquires new capabilities. A capability is anything that extends what the swarm can do: a model, a skill, a plugin, a tool, a third-party service, a connected platform, a reusable workflow method, or an automation component not already in production.

Capabilities are not adopted on impulse. They must be evaluated, approved by the human, registered, scoped, and versioned before they enter production use. This prevents capability sprawl, undeclared dependencies, unreviewed risk surface, and the drift that comes from each agent improvising new tools without organizational awareness.

**Why this matters:**
- An unapproved capability can introduce security exposure, cost obligations, data routing risks, or reliability assumptions that were never reviewed
- A capability in use but not registered cannot be maintained, deprecated, or governed
- Repeated improvisation of the same task pattern is a signal to evaluate whether a capability should be formally acquired
- Agents cannot acquire capabilities on their own authority — they may identify and recommend; the human decides

**This document governs:**
- What counts as a capability subject to this policy
- How capabilities are discovered, proposed, and evaluated
- The approval gates required before production adoption
- How capabilities are registered, versioned, and scoped
- How risky capabilities receive stronger gates
- How legacy and improvised capabilities are brought into compliance
- How capabilities are retired

**What this document does not cover:**
- Workflow definitions (reusable task execution patterns) — D5.6-WORKFLOW_LIBRARY.md
- Secrets and credential management for capabilities — D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md
- Automation class and risk level for an individual task — D1.8-AUTOMATION_POLICY.md
- Specific experiment records — D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md

---

## 2. Scope

This policy applies to all capability additions, whether initiated by the human or surfaced by agents. It covers:

| Capability Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| AI models | Language models, embedding models, image models, specialized inference models |
| Agent skills / plugins | Tool-use plugins, function call schemas, capability extensions added to an agent's available toolkit |
| Automation tools | Scripts, cron jobs, API wrappers, programmatic integrations built by the swarm |
| Third-party services | External APIs, SaaS platforms, data providers, notification services |
| Connected platforms | Social channels, e-commerce platforms, knowledge bases, data pipelines |
| Reusable workflow methods | Prompt templates, evaluation frameworks, data transformation patterns used repeatedly across tasks |
| Infrastructure components | Databases, queues, file systems, containers used as capability substrate |

A capability is subject to this policy if adopting it would:
- Change what the swarm can do
- Create an external dependency
- Introduce a cost obligation (actual or potential)
- Expose the organization's data or credentials to a new surface
- Require configuration, maintenance, or operational attention

---

## 3. Policy Principles

1. **Agents recommend; humans decide.** No agent may adopt a capability into production use on its own authority. Agents may identify candidates, draft proposals, run experiments, and recommend adoption — but the human approves before any capability is used in production.

2. **Prefer approved capabilities over improvisation.** When an approved capability can fulfill a task requirement, it is used. Improvising a new approach when an approved one exists is a deviation from policy and must be flagged. (Source: D5.6 §4.1)

3. **Every production capability is registered.** A capability in use that is not in the capability register is non-compliant. Agents are responsible for flagging unregistered capabilities they discover in active use.

4. **Scope is declared, not inferred.** Every registered capability has a declared scope: which agents may use it, for which task classes, under what conditions. Using a capability outside its declared scope requires a scope update, which is treated as a new acquisition decision.

5. **Risky capabilities require stronger gates.** Capabilities that touch financial data, credentials, external write access, or user data are Risk Class 3 or higher. They do not enter production on the same path as low-risk utilities.

6. **Acquisition is reversible; operational dependency is not.** Evaluate and register before use. Once a capability is embedded in live workflows, reversing it is costly. The right time to apply scrutiny is before adoption, not after.

7. **Capability sprawl is a failure mode.** More is not better. Each new capability adds maintenance surface, potential failure points, and organizational complexity. Acquisitions are justified by demonstrated need, not speculative future use.

8. **Cost is always surfaced.** Any capability with a variable, usage-based, or subscription cost must have that cost declared at acquisition time and monitored as defined in D1.8 §16 (financial track). No capability with unknown or uncapped cost enters production without an explicit cost ceiling approved by the human.

9. **Experiments are not production.** Running a capability in an experiment (D5.4) is not the same as adopting it for production use. An experiment that succeeds must still pass through the acquisition gates in §7 before production deployment.

10. **Legacy capabilities are not exempt.** Capabilities that predate this policy are subject to retroactive registration and review. Until reviewed, they are treated as pending approval — agents should flag them and the human should schedule review.

---

## 4. Capability Categories

Capabilities are classified by their primary function. This classification determines which evaluation checklist applies and what the default risk class is.

| Category | Description | Default Risk Class |
|---|---|---|
| CAP-MOD | AI model — inference, embedding, classification | 2 (Medium) |
| CAP-SKL | Agent skill or plugin — extends an agent's tool palette | 2 (Medium) |
| CAP-AUT | Automation tool — script, API wrapper, cron component | 1–3 (based on access scope) |
| CAP-SVC | Third-party service — external API or SaaS dependency | 2–3 (based on data exposure) |
| CAP-PLT | Connected platform — social, commerce, data pipeline | 3 (High) — external write access assumed |
| CAP-WFM | Workflow method — prompt template, evaluation framework, reusable pattern | 1 (Low) |
| CAP-INF | Infrastructure component — DB, queue, storage, container | 2–3 (based on data sensitivity) |

Risk class may be elevated based on evaluation findings. It may not be lowered below the default for its category without explicit justification in the capability record.

**Known candidate in the acquisition pipeline:**

| Candidate | Category | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perplexity (live research / UI-pilot / device / orchestration assist) | CAP-SVC | Candidate — D5.7 CAP-006 | Four candidate roles under evaluation: (1) live research lane — live web retrieval, current-information queries; (2) preferred UI-pilot lane — operating apps, browsers, chatboxes, and devices through their native interface, including indirect operation of Cursor Claude chat without requiring direct API integration (D1.5 §15.5); (3) access-reduction/device lane — device automation assistance, computer-use fallback for access tasks; (4) orchestration assist lane — real-time context retrieval during planning. Risk Class 2–3 (data exposure assessment pending; elevated to 3 if device-lane use is activated). Evaluation: TASK-013. Scope decision: TASK-014. Not activated at Phase 1. |

---

## 5. Acquisition Sources

Capabilities may be surfaced through any of the following sources:

### 5.1 X Research Pipeline
The X research pipeline (D1.10) may identify capabilities worth evaluating. Signal types `capability` and `architecture` in D1.10 §7 are the primary capability-discovery signals. A research finding that reaches the output stage (D1.10 §10) becomes a capability candidate, not an automatic acquisition.

### 5.2 Experiment Results
An experiment in D5.4 may demonstrate the value of a new capability and recommend adoption. Successful experiment results create a capability candidate. The experiment record is linked from the capability record at acquisition time.

### 5.3 Human Intuition and Directive
The human may identify a capability worth evaluating based on external awareness, industry exposure, or organizational need. Human-initiated candidates skip the candidate surfacing stage and go directly to evaluation.

### 5.4 Repeated Work Patterns (Internal Signal)
When the swarm repeatedly improvises the same capability (using an unapproved tool for the same class of work 3+ times), this is a signal that a formal acquisition may be warranted. Conductor surfaces this alongside the workflow candidate signal in §5.3 of D5.6. The human decides whether to acquire the capability or leave it ad-hoc.

### 5.5 Legacy Asset Discovery
Capabilities already in use but not registered are discovered through legacy asset review (D5.x). These are surfaced to the human for retroactive evaluation and either registered or retired.

---

## 6. Evaluation Stages

Every capability candidate passes through these stages before approval is requested.

### 6.1 Stage 1 — Candidate Identification
- Source is identified (§5 above)
- Capability category assigned (§4)
- Initial risk class assigned
- A capability proposal stub is created in the capability register (status: `candidate`)

### 6.2 Stage 2 — Feasibility and Fit Assessment
- Does this capability solve a real, demonstrated need? (not speculative)
- Does an approved capability already cover this need? (if yes, stop — use the existing one)
- Is the source credible? (if CAP-MOD or CAP-SVC: is the provider established? is the API stable?)
- What is the cost structure? (free tier, usage-based, subscription — with ceiling estimate)

### 6.3 Stage 3 — Risk and Dependency Analysis
- What external dependencies does this introduce?
- What data is shared with the capability (credentials, user data, task content)?
- What is the failure mode if this capability becomes unavailable?
- Does this create a single point of failure in any workflow?
- Does this touch financial, credential, or user-facing systems? (elevates risk class)

### 6.4 Stage 4 — Experiment (Optional but Recommended)
For CAP-MOD, CAP-SKL, CAP-SVC, and CAP-PLT: a bounded experiment is recommended before approval. The experiment is filed in D5.4. Results are linked to the capability proposal.

For CAP-WFM and low-risk CAP-AUT: a brief trial may substitute for a formal experiment.

### 6.5 Stage 5 — Adoption Proposal
A formal adoption proposal is drafted and submitted to the human. Format defined in §7.2.

---

## 7. Approval Gates

### 7.1 Risk-Gated Approval Requirements

| Risk Class | Approval Required | Experiment Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Low) | Human acknowledgement | No | Proposal posted in Telegram; human confirms |
| 2 (Medium) | Human explicit approval | Recommended | Proposal in Mission Control; human responds in Telegram or MC |
| 3 (High) | Human explicit approval + review period | Yes | Minimum 48-hour review window before activation |
| 4 (Critical) | Human explicit approval + documented risk acceptance | Yes | Written acknowledgement in Mission Control required; no time pressure |

Risk Class 4 is reserved for capabilities with financial write access, credential management scope, or direct data exfiltration risk. These are rare and treated as organizational-level decisions, not operational ones.

### 7.2 Adoption Proposal Format

Every proposal submitted for approval must include:

```
---
id: CAP-<sequential number>
date: YYYY-MM-DD
proposed_by: human | bridge | brain | forge
category: CAP-MOD | CAP-SKL | CAP-AUT | CAP-SVC | CAP-PLT | CAP-WFM | CAP-INF
name: <short human-readable name>
risk_class: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4
source: X-research | experiment | human | internal-pattern | legacy
experiment_ref: <D5.4 experiment ID or null>
---

## What This Is
<1–2 sentence description of the capability>

## Why It Is Needed
<Demonstrated need — reference the task class or pattern that surfaced this>

## What It Can Do
<Scope of function — what it can do AND what it explicitly cannot do in this organization>

## What It Costs
<Cost structure: free / usage-based (with ceiling estimate) / subscription (rate) / infrastructure cost>

## Dependencies Introduced
<External services, credentials, or infrastructure it depends on>

## Data Exposure
<What data the capability touches, where it is sent, and under what conditions>

## Failure Mode
<What breaks if this capability becomes unavailable>

## Alternatives Considered
<Why existing approved capabilities do not cover this need>

## Proposed Scope
<Which agents may use it, for which task classes, under what conditions>

## Recommended Activation Gate
<What must be true before this is used in production (credentials registered, test passed, etc.)>
```

### 7.3 Approval Decision

The human responds with one of:
- **Approve** — capability may be registered and activated per the declared scope
- **Approve with conditions** — capability may be registered with modified scope or additional gate
- **Defer** — not now; re-surface at a defined milestone
- **Decline** — not for this organization; record the reason

The decision is recorded in the capability register. Declined capabilities retain their record with status `declined` — the reasoning is preserved for future reference.

---

## 8. Registration and Versioning Policy

### 8.1 Capability Register

The capability register is maintained in Mission Control at **D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md** (see §16 Priority 1). Every production capability has an entry. The register is the source of truth for what the organization is currently running.

**Relationship to D5.2-SKILL-REGISTRY.md:** D5.2 is a specialized index for agent skills and plugins (CAP-SKL category only). D5.7 is the master register covering all capability categories. When a skill or plugin is registered, it has a full record in D5.7 and a reference entry in D5.2 for skill-specific metadata (invocation pattern, agent binding, activation command). D5.7 is authoritative for approval status and compliance fields. D5.2 is authoritative for operational skill invocation metadata. Do not treat them as duplicates — they serve different purposes.

**Write authority:** D5.7 is an operational Mission Control registry, not a D-series policy document. Bridge (Conductor) may update the following fields within its approved operational scope: `status` (transitions to `active` or `deprecated`), `last_reviewed`, `review_due`, `notes`, and usage data for cost tracking. Bridge may not change `approved_by`, `approval_date`, `risk_class`, `authorized_agents`, or `authorized_task_classes` without a new human approval decision. Bridge's operational register access does not affect human-only authority over capability approval and scope.

Required fields for every registered capability:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `id` | CAP-### sequential |
| `name` | Short human-readable name |
| `category` | From §4 |
| `status` | `candidate` / `approved` / `active` / `deprecated` / `retired` / `declined` |
| `risk_class` | 1–4 |
| `version` | Integer, starting at 1 |
| `approved_by` | `human` |
| `approval_date` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `authorized_agents` | Which agents may use it |
| `authorized_task_classes` | Which task classes it may be used in |
| `cost_ceiling` | Approved monthly cost limit (null if free) |
| `experiment_ref` | D5.4 experiment ID, or null |
| `source_ref` | D1.10 research packet ID, or null |
| `last_reviewed` | YYYY-MM-DD |
| `review_due` | YYYY-MM-DD (based on §8.3) |
| `notes` | Any conditions, restrictions, or known issues |

### 8.2 Versioning

When a registered capability changes in a way that affects its scope, risk class, cost, or authorized use:
- The version number increments
- The prior version is retained in the record under `change_notes`
- If the change elevates the risk class or expands scope, re-approval is required before the new version is used in production

Routine updates (e.g. SDK patch version, API endpoint change with no behavioral difference) do not require re-approval — they are recorded as a version note.

### 8.3 Review Cadence

Every active capability is reviewed on a schedule based on its risk class:

| Risk Class | Review Cadence |
|---|---|
| 1 | Annual |
| 2 | 6-month |
| 3 | Quarterly |
| 4 | Monthly |

Reviews confirm the capability is still needed, still compliant, still within cost bounds, and that no better alternative has emerged. Review is triggered by the capability register's `review_due` field surfaced in the weekly digest.

---

## 9. Production Adoption Policy

### 9.1 Activation Gate

An approved capability is not automatically active. Before first use in production:
1. Credentials and tokens registered in D1.14 (if applicable)
2. Scope confirmed with the authorized agents
3. A test task executed and verified
4. The capability record updated to `status: active` with activation date

An agent that attempts to use an approved-but-not-yet-activated capability must halt and surface the gap to bridge.

### 9.2 Scope Enforcement

Agents use capabilities only within their declared scope. A capability authorized for `task_class: research` is not used for `task_class: content-publishing` without a scope update. Scope creep discovered in a task record must be flagged and the capability record corrected before the out-of-scope use continues.

### 9.3 Cost Monitoring

For capabilities with usage-based or subscription costs, bridge monitors actual vs. ceiling monthly. If usage approaches 80% of the declared cost ceiling, a notice goes into the daily digest. If it hits 100%, automation halts and an escalation fires. (Source: D1.8 §16 financial track)

### 9.4 Dependency Cascade Awareness

When a capability is retired, any workflows or task classes that depend on it are identified before retirement is complete. Dependent workflows must be updated or blocked before the capability is deactivated. Bridge maintains the dependency map in the capability register.

---

## 10. Workflow and Method Acquisition Policy

### 10.1 Reusable Workflow Methods (CAP-WFM)

A workflow method is a reusable approach to a class of work: a prompt template, an evaluation rubric, a data transformation pattern, a multi-step decision framework. These are not the same as workflow definitions in D5.6 (which define execution sequences for recurring tasks) — they are the tools and techniques used within those sequences.

CAP-WFM capabilities are lower risk by default (Risk Class 1) because they do not introduce external dependencies. They are still subject to registration if they become consistently used across multiple task classes.

### 10.2 Prefer Registered Methods

When a registered CAP-WFM exists that covers a task requirement, it is used. Creating a new ad-hoc method when a registered one applies is flagged as a deviation in the task record.

### 10.3 Method Candidate Surface

When the same prompt template, evaluation rubric, or transformation pattern is used 3+ times by any agent, it becomes a CAP-WFM candidate. Conductor surfaces it the same way it surfaces workflow candidates (D5.6 §5.3).

---

## 11. Legacy Capability Migration Policy

### 11.1 Definition

A legacy capability is any capability in use that predates this policy or that was adopted without going through the acquisition gates defined here. This includes:
- Tools or APIs integrated before the organization formalized
- Skills or plugins that were added ad-hoc and never registered
- Third-party services that agents were given access to without formal review

### 11.2 Discovery and Flagging

Agents encountering an unregistered capability in active use must:
1. Record it in the task record as `unregistered_capability_detected: [name]`
2. Include it in the next daily digest
3. Not cease using it immediately if ceasing would break an active workflow — continue use pending review, but flag it

### 11.3 Review Outcome

After discovery, the human schedules a retroactive review using the evaluation stages in §6 (stages 2–5 are sufficient; stage 1 is already complete by discovery). The outcome is one of:
- **Register and continue** — passes retroactive review; added to capability register with current version
- **Register with restrictions** — passes with conditions; scope reduced or usage rules added
- **Retire** — does not pass; retirement timeline and workflow impact assessed per §13

Legacy capabilities that are not reviewed within 90 days of discovery are treated as non-compliant and bridge escalates.

---

## 12. Risk Classification for Capabilities

### 12.1 Risk Class Assignment

Risk class is assigned at candidate identification (§6.1) based on category defaults (§4) and the factors below. It may only be elevated during evaluation — not lowered without justification in the record.

**Factors that elevate risk class:**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| External write access (write to APIs, post to platforms) | +1 minimum |
| Financial data access or financial transaction capability | Automatically Risk Class 4 |
| Credential or secret management scope | +1 minimum |
| PII or user data exposure | +1 minimum |
| Single point of failure in a critical workflow | +1 |
| Usage-based cost with no ceiling | +1 |
| Beta / unstable API from an unestablished provider | +1 |

### 12.2 Risk Class Definitions

| Class | Name | Gate | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low | Human acknowledgement | Read-only utility, prompt template, internal tool with no external dependency |
| 2 | Medium | Human explicit approval | Language model API, internal automation tool, read-only external API |
| 3 | High | Explicit approval + 48h review + experiment | External write API, platform integration, service with significant cost potential |
| 4 | Critical | Explicit approval + documented risk acceptance + written MC record | Financial transaction capability, credential management, irreversible external action scope |

### 12.3 Risk Class Is Not Automation Class

Risk class (§12.2) describes the acquisition gate required. Automation class (D1.8 §4) describes how the capability is executed once approved. They are independent. A Risk Class 2 capability may be executed as a Class 1 (cron-scheduled) automation or a Class 4 (delegated multi-step) automation, depending on how it is used.

---

## 13. Retirement and Deactivation Policy

### 13.1 Retirement Triggers

A capability is retired when:
- It is no longer used in any active workflow or task class
- It has been superseded by a better-approved alternative
- Its provider has deprecated it or the integration is no longer maintainable
- The human explicitly retires it
- It fails retroactive review (§11.3) and the decision is "retire"

### 13.2 Retirement Process

Retirement follows this sequence:
1. Identify all workflows and task classes that depend on the capability
2. Update or block dependent workflows before deactivation
3. Update the capability register: `status: retired`, add `retirement_date` and `retirement_reason`
4. Remove credentials from D1.14 per the secrets deactivation process
5. Confirm no agent is calling the capability post-retirement (one-cycle verification)

A retired capability is not deleted from the register. It is marked retired and retained for audit.

### 13.3 Deprecation vs. Retirement

A capability is `deprecated` when it is still functional but should no longer be used for new work (a better alternative now exists). Existing uses may continue during a transition period. It is `retired` when it is fully deactivated and no agent may use it. The transition from deprecated to retired requires a workflow migration plan.

---

## 14. Human and Agent Roles in Capability Acquisition

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| Human | Approves all production capability adoptions; sets risk tolerance; reviews high-risk capabilities; retires capabilities |
| Bridge (Conductor) | Surfaces capability candidates from internal patterns; manages the capability register; monitors cost ceilings; escalates unregistered capabilities; tracks review due dates |
| Brain (Archivist) | Archives experiment results and capability records to canonical memory; provides historical capability context during evaluation |
| Forge | Executes capability experiments (D5.4); reports findings; flags unregistered capabilities discovered in task execution |
| Any agent | May identify and report a capability candidate; may not approve, adopt, or register a capability without human approval |

**The boundary:** Agents are the discovery and execution layer. The human is the acquisition authority. An agent that adopts an unapproved capability in production — regardless of how confident it is in the value — has violated this policy.

---

## 15. Blocked / TBD

| Item | Blocked On | Expected Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md creation | File not yet created; D5.7 slot confirmed in D1.1 §6 | Phase 1 setup; register created alongside task register |
| First capability candidates | No operations running yet; no patterns observed | Phase 1 after 2–4 weeks of operation |
| CAP-MOD candidates | No models currently authorized beyond base bridge capability | Phase 1 when model requirements emerge |
| CAP-SVC candidates | No third-party services configured yet | As specific services are needed |
| Legacy capability inventory | Operations not yet started; no capabilities to inventory | Phase 1 after setup; initial inventory at Phase 1 close |
| Cost monitoring integration | Bridge not yet connected to usage APIs | Phase 2 after bridge is operational and APIs are configured |
| Review cadence automation | Capability register not yet built; cron infrastructure not yet running | Phase 2 after capability register and cron infrastructure are online |
| CAP-PLT policy refinement (platform-specific rules) | No platform integrations active yet | Refined as platforms are acquired (D1.10 commerce-signal track) |

---

## 16. Immediate Priorities

1. **Create D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md during Phase 1 setup** — The register is the backbone of this policy. Without a defined home in Mission Control, capabilities cannot be formally tracked. Create D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md and register it as a writable surface for bridge per the write authority rules in §8.1. D5.7 must exist before any capability is moved from `candidate` to `approved`. (Source: §8.1)

2. **Create an initial legacy capability inventory at Phase 1 start** — Before operations begin, list every tool, API, model, and service the swarm will use at Day 1. Retroactively register all of them as founding-state capabilities. This prevents the entire starting configuration from being treated as "pending review." (Source: §11)

3. **Set cost ceilings for any usage-based services before activation** — Any capability with variable cost must have a human-approved cost ceiling before it goes active. This is a prerequisite step, not a post-activation concern. (Source: §9.3, D1.8 §16)

4. **Link D1.10 research pipeline to capability candidate surface** — When a D1.10 research finding is tagged `signal_type: capability` or `architecture`, the output stage should automatically create a capability candidate stub in the register. This connection should be confirmed when both D1.10 Mode 2 and the capability register are operational. (Source: §5.1)

5. **Confirm bridge has write access to the capability register** — Bridge (Conductor) manages the register, updates status fields, monitors review due dates, and escalates unregistered capabilities. This access path must be confirmed as part of the Mission Control access policy resolution (D1.3 MC-ACC-03). (Source: §14)

---

_This policy exists because the gap between "we tried it and it worked" and "we adopted it" is where organizational debt accumulates. Every capability that enters production without a record becomes invisible overhead. Register what you use. Use what you register._

docs.title: D1.13 — ENV_CONTROL
docs.content:
# D1.13 — ENV_CONTROL
**Autonomous AI Organization — Environment Control, Configuration Governance, and Drift Prevention**
_Last updated: 2026-03-11 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines how environments are controlled across the organization. An environment is the totality of configuration, state, services, and constraints on a machine or within a context that determines how work is executed there.

Without deliberate environment control, the swarm accumulates invisible assumptions. Agents write one-off scripts that become de facto infrastructure. Config changes made during debugging persist silently. Credentials leak across boundaries. A method that worked yesterday fails today because something changed without a record. None of this shows up as an error until it does — loudly, in production.

Environment control exists to make the swarm's execution consistent, reproducible, and auditable. A workflow that works in production today should work in production next month — because the environment is defined, not improvised.

**This document governs:**
- What environment types exist and what each permits
- Which machines participate in which environments
- What is authoritative vs. local-only state
- How configuration is layered and constrained
- What must be version-controlled
- How changes move from experiment to production
- What counts as a healthy machine baseline
- What agents may change autonomously and what they may not
- How reusable methods and workflows become environment assets
- How drift is detected and corrected

**What this document does not cover:**
- Specific secrets, tokens, and credentials — D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md
- How to set up or operate machines — D2.x runbooks
- Capability approval and registration — D1.12-CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY.md
- Workflow definition and promotion — D5.6-WORKFLOW_LIBRARY.md
- Agent task routing and message formats — D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md

---

## 2. Scope

This policy applies to all machines in the organization and all configuration surfaces those machines expose:

| Surface | What It Covers |
|---|---|
| Machine configuration | OS-level settings, startup services, network config, sleep/power policy |
| Agent configuration | OpenClaw workspace settings, model routing config, tool scope definitions |
| Runtime state | Active services, running processes, open connections |
| Credential surfaces | API keys, tokens, and access credentials (policy defined here; management in D1.14) |
| Version-controlled assets | Mission Control documents, workflow definitions, config files, registered methods |
| Operational registries | Task register, capability register, workflow library, delegation status |

All five machines are in scope: Mac Mini, Desktop, GPU3060, MacBook Air, and Pixel/Seeker (Phase 5).

---

## 3. Environment Control Principles

1. **Consistency beats convenience.** An approach that works once is not worth adopting unless it can be made repeatable. Shortcuts that bypass defined config layers are not conveniences — they are undeclared dependencies.

2. **Production is boring on purpose.** Production environments change slowly, deliberately, and with proof. Novelty belongs in experimental environments. Production's job is to be reliable, not interesting.

3. **Experiment first, promote later.** No new capability, config change, or method goes directly from idea to production. It passes through an experimental or sandbox environment and earns promotion through demonstrated stability. The risk of getting this wrong is not worth the time saved by skipping it.

4. **One authoritative source per state type.** For any given kind of state — policies, canonical memory, operational task state, capability approvals — exactly one location is authoritative. Local copies and caches are derivatives. When there is a conflict, the authoritative source wins.

5. **Reusable methods are environment assets.** A method, prompt template, workflow, or build pattern that has been used successfully is an organizational asset. It belongs in a registered location (D5.6 or D5.7), not only in the memory of the agent that created it. Environment control is what makes method reuse work reliably across runs, machines, and time.

6. **No silent drift.** A change to the environment that is not recorded did not happen in an auditable sense. If an agent updates a config value, installs a dependency, or enables a service, that change is logged. If it is not loggable, it is not within the agent's authority to make.

7. **Self-managed does not mean self-redefining.** Agents pull and execute approved work without per-task human prompting. They do not use that autonomy to redefine their own environment boundaries, expand their own access scope, or declare a new environment type. Environment changes are proposed, approved, and applied through the change process. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 15, D1.8 §3)

8. **Recovery must be possible from documentation alone.** Any environment that cannot be rebuilt from version-controlled documentation and approved config is fragile. If the only record of how something was configured is the running machine, the organization has an unacceptable dependency on that machine's continuous health.

9. **Config debt is technical debt.** Undocumented configuration, unregistered capabilities, and ad-hoc overrides accumulate silently. Each one is a future failure point. Environment control is how the organization avoids this class of debt.

---

## 4. Environment Types

The organization operates four environment types. Each has defined permitted and forbidden activities. All work happens in one of these types — there is no undefined fifth environment.

**Note on external managed services:** Third-party external services (e.g. Perplexity, Telegram, Anthropic API) are not environment types — they are callable capabilities governed by D1.12 and registered in D5.7. These services run in the provider's environment; the organization's environment boundary applies to how credentials are managed (D1.14) and how outputs are handled within the org's systems. Invoking an external service from within a production task does not change the environment type of that task. The capability must be approved and registered before production use; this is a capability governance requirement, not an environment control question.

### 4.1 Production

**What it is:** The live operating environment where the swarm runs real tasks, manages real state, and produces real outputs. The organization's canonical record, operational task queue, and active agent services run here.

**Permitted:**
- Approved, registered capabilities executed within their declared scope
- Tasks at or above Autonomy Level 2 (per D1.8 §4)
- Changes applied through the promotion path (§9 below)
- Operational registry updates within bridge's declared write authority (D1.12 §8.1)

**Forbidden:**
- Experimental or untested configuration
- Test credentials or placeholder secrets
- Any change not traceable to an approved workflow, capability, or human directive
- Changes that have not passed through a sandbox or experimental review
- Silent dependency installations

**Governing documents:** D1.8, D1.9, D1.12, D1.14, and all D1.x policy docs

### 4.2 Sandbox

**What it is:** A controlled environment that closely mirrors production but is isolated from live state and real credentials. Used for validating changes before production promotion. Inputs and outputs are bounded — sandbox results do not affect real external systems.

**Permitted:**
- Validating promoted candidates from experimental (see §9)
- Integration testing of new capabilities or config changes
- Rehearsal of workflows before production activation
- Model evaluation with production-equivalent (but not real) workloads

**Forbidden:**
- Production secrets or real credentials
- Connection to live external services (read-only access may be approved with explicit scope)
- Permanent state — sandbox state is expected to be cleared between sessions

**Governing documents:** D1.8, D1.12, D5.4

### 4.3 Dev

**What it is:** A local environment for writing, editing, and testing new code, documents, agent config, or workflow definitions. Dev work is authored by the human or by agents operating under explicit dev-context task scope. No real agent tasks run here by default.

**Permitted:**
- Document authoring and editing
- Code writing, linting, and unit testing
- Agent config drafting before sandbox validation
- Prompt template drafting before registration as CAP-WFM

**Forbidden:**
- Real agent task execution
- Connection to production registries or canonical memory
- Production credentials

**Governing documents:** D1.15-CHANGE_CONTROL.md (to be written), D1.8

### 4.4 Experimental

**What it is:** An unconstrained workspace for trying new capabilities, models, methods, and approaches. Outputs are ephemeral until adoption is decided. Failure is expected and acceptable. Experimental work runs on GPU3060 by default. It does not affect production state.

**Permitted:**
- Any new capability, model, or method that has not yet been evaluated
- Experiments registered in D5.4
- Novel approaches to known tasks, with results compared against existing methods
- Agent behavior under unusual inputs (stress, edge cases, adversarial prompts)

**Forbidden:**
- Real credentials or production secrets
- Adoption into production without passing through the promotion path (§9)
- Running experimental code directly on production machines

**Governing documents:** D5.4-EXPERIMENT-REGISTRY.md, D1.12 §6

---

## 5. Machine-to-Environment Mapping

Each machine has a primary environment role and defined constraints on secondary environment hosting. This mapping is authoritative for environment policy. Changes to these assignments require an update to both this document and D1.2.

| Machine | Primary Environment | Secondary Allowed | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mac Mini | Production | None | Gateway stability is critical — no experiments, no dev testing, no secondary environments that could affect bridge uptime |
| Desktop | Production | Sandbox (isolated path) | Canonical memory is production; a separate non-overlapping directory path may host sandbox work for brain research tasks — but only with explicit path isolation |
| GPU3060 | Experimental / Sandbox | Dev | Primary experiment surface; safe to fail here; promotes outputs to production only after sandbox validation |
| MacBook Air | Dev | None | Human authoring and control surface; not a worker node; not a server; org does not depend on it |
| Pixel / Seeker | Edge (Phase 5) | None currently | Not production agents; not experiment surfaces; edge task receipts only; environment role TBD at Phase 5 design |

**Mac Mini constraint restated:** The Mac Mini runs bridge in production at all times. Nothing runs on the Mac Mini that could degrade gateway responsiveness, consume unexpected memory, or alter network configuration without explicit approval. This is a hard constraint, not a preference. (Source: D1.2 §13)

**Desktop constraint:** The Desktop hosts canonical memory — the organization's most critical durable state. Any experimental or sandbox work on the Desktop must run in a fully isolated directory hierarchy with no shared paths, no shared credentials, and no possibility of write access to canonical memory locations.

---

## 6. Authoritative Sources of State

For every class of state, exactly one location is authoritative. Local copies are derivatives. In any conflict, the authoritative source wins.

| State Class | Authoritative Location | Local Cache Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Org policies (D1.x) | Mission Control: `docs/` on Mac Mini | No local cache — always read from source |
| Mission Control host | Mac Mini: `~/Cursor Projects/mission-control/` | MacBook Air has working copy; Mac Mini copy is authoritative |
| Canonical memory | Desktop (brain) — path TBD in Phase 2 | Agents may cache read results in working memory; cannot write to canonical memory directly |
| Operational memory (task state) | Mac Mini (bridge) — `~/.openclaw/` and Mission Control task register | Other agents read via bridge; no direct writes |
| Working memory | GPU3060 (forge) — local to forge | Local only; ephemeral; not promoted to canonical without explicit submission to Archivist |
| Capability register (D5.7) | Mission Control: `registry/D5.7-CAPABILITY-REGISTER.md` | Bridge may cache for read; status field updates must write back to source |
| Workflow library (D5.6) | Mission Control: `docs/D5.6-WORKFLOW_LIBRARY.md` | Agents read from source; no local forks |
| Delegation status | Mission Control: autonomy register (D1.8 §9.1) | Bridge reads; human sets; no agent promotes its own delegation level |
| Secrets and credentials | D1.14 — registered secret store (path TBD) | Loaded into process memory at runtime; never written to task records, logs, or registries |
| Agent config | Per-machine OpenClaw workspace + Mission Control config reference | Local override only within declared agent-local layer (see §7) |

**Local cache rule:** A local cache is always a read-through derivative of the authoritative source. Agents may cache for performance but must validate against source on divergence. A cache that becomes the de facto source of record — because the authoritative source was not updated — is a silent drift condition.

---

## 7. Configuration Layering Policy

Configuration is layered. Higher layers constrain lower layers. Lower layers may customize within the space allowed by the layer above — they may never override or violate a higher-layer constraint.

```
Layer 1 — Org-wide policy
    Defined in D1.x documents and Mission Control registries.
    Governs all machines, all agents, all tasks.
    Human sets. Agents read. Agents may not redefine.

Layer 2 — Machine-local config
    Settings specific to a single machine: LAN IP, model path, service startup list,
    local inference parameters, resource caps.
    Set during machine setup (D2.x runbooks). Recorded in Mission Control.
    Bridge may update within approved scope; changes logged.

Layer 3 — Agent-local config
    Settings specific to one agent's runtime: workspace path, model routing preference,
    task class defaults, tool access list.
    Defined in agent's OpenClaw workspace config.
    Must not exceed what Layer 1 and Layer 2 permit.
    Agent may tune within approved parameters; changes logged.

Layer 4 — Task-local overrides
    Per-task adjustments: specific model override, timeout extension, resource reservation.
    Applied at task packet level. Must be declared in the task record.
    May not violate Layer 1 policy (risk class, financial controls, escalation triggers).
    Ephemeral — do not persist beyond task completion.
```

**Rules for all layers:**
- Higher layers constrain lower. A task-local override cannot circumvent an org-wide policy.
- Agents may not invent undeclared config layers. There are four. Adding a fifth requires a change to this document, approved by the human.
- A config value with no declared layer assignment belongs at Layer 1 until the human classifies it.
- Layer conflicts are resolved upward: if Layer 4 and Layer 1 disagree, Layer 1 wins and bridge escalates the conflict.

---

## 8. Version Control Policy

### 8.1 Mission Control as the Tracked Workspace

Mission Control at `~/Cursor Projects/mission-control/` on the Mac Mini is a git repository. All documents, registries, workflow definitions, and tracked config files in that workspace are version-controlled. The commit history is the organization's operational audit trail.

### 8.2 What Must Be Committed

The following must be committed to version control and must not exist only as untracked files:

- All D1.x–D5.x documents
- Task register entries (as they are created or updated)
- Capability register entries (D5.7)
- Workflow library entries (D5.6)
- Delegation status register
- Machine baseline declarations (see §10)
- Automation cron definitions (D1.8 §9.1)
- Proof artifact references (not the artifacts themselves — the references to them)
- Any approved configuration change that affects environment scope

### 8.3 What Must Never Be Committed

- Secrets, API keys, tokens, or credentials of any kind
- Temporary debug output or raw model responses
- Personal identifiers or private human data
- Large binary files (model weights, databases — these are referenced, not stored)
- Local scratch work that has not been reviewed and promoted (working notes go in working memory, not in tracked docs)

Secrets committed to the repo — even briefly — are treated as compromised and must be rotated. History rewrites to remove secrets from git history are performed before the branch is merged, not after.

### 8.4 Config Changes Require Document Update First

If a config change affects the declared behavior of any D-series document, the document is updated first and committed before the config change is applied. The commit message references the document section being implemented. This prevents the running environment from diverging silently from its documented state. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 1, D1.2 guardrail 6)

### 8.5 Reusable Methods Must Be Tracked

A prompt template, evaluation rubric, or workflow method that has been used successfully is not informally remembered — it is registered (as CAP-WFM in D5.7 or as a workflow in D5.6) and committed. Methods that exist only in agent working memory or in a chat thread are not organization assets. They are personal knowledge that will be lost. (Source: D1.12 §10)

---

## 9. Environment Promotion Policy

Work moves through environments in order. Riskier changes require more steps. No change skips the sequence without explicit human approval and documented justification.

### 9.1 Promotion Path

```
Experimental → Sandbox → Production

For low-risk changes (Risk Class 1, no external dependencies):
    Experimental → Production (abbreviated path, human acknowledgement required)

For medium-risk changes (Risk Class 2):
    Experimental → Sandbox → Production (full path, explicit approval required)

For high-risk and critical changes (Risk Class 3–4):
    Experimental → Sandbox → 48h hold → Production
    (written approval + experiment record + human sign-off on each transition)
```

### 9.2 Promotion Requirements Per Transition

**Experimental → Sandbox:**
- Experiment record exists in D5.4
- Experiment result documents that the approach works as intended
- Risk class has been assigned
- No production credentials used

**Sandbox → Production:**
- Sandbox validation confirmed and documented
- Adoption proposal submitted and approved per D1.12 §7 (if a new capability)
- Any new config changes committed to version control
- Rollback path identified (what is done if the production change fails)
- For Risk Class 3+: 48-hour hold period completed, human has explicitly signed off

### 9.3 Rollback Expectation

Every production change must have a declared rollback path before it is applied. "We will figure it out if something breaks" is not a rollback path. The rollback is documented in the task record and the capability/workflow entry before the change is applied.

### 9.4 No Direct Experiment-to-Production for Risk Class 2+

An experiment that produces a working result is a candidate for adoption — not an approved production change. The result triggers the adoption proposal process (D1.12 §6). The proposal is approved. The sandbox run is conducted. Production activation follows. The excitement of a successful experiment is not a reason to skip the path.

---

## 10. Machine Baseline State Policy

A baseline is the defined state a machine should be in during normal operations. Departure from baseline is drift. Drift is flagged. Every machine has an explicit baseline declaration committed to Mission Control.

### 10.1 Mac Mini Baseline

| Property | Baseline State |
|---|---|
| Power state | Always on. Display off acceptable. Sleep disabled. |
| On reboot | OpenClaw gateway starts automatically via LaunchAgent. |
| Required services | OpenClaw gateway (port 18789, loopback or LAN), SSH daemon, Screen Sharing daemon |
| Background load | Only declared services. No experiment processes, no dev environments, no heavy inference in parallel with gateway tasks. |
| Network | Ethernet primary (stable DHCP reservation, Phase 1+ target); Wi-Fi as backup. |
| Local LLM | Required and running (model TBD — registered when selected per D1.6). |
| Drift indicators | Gateway not responding, sleep state active, undeclared process consuming > 10% CPU sustained |

### 10.2 Desktop (Brain) Baseline

| Property | Baseline State |
|---|---|
| Power state | Always on. Display off acceptable. |
| On reboot | brain agent starts and confirms connection to bridge gateway. |
| Required services | OpenClaw brain agent runtime, local LLM inference service, canonical memory accessibility check |
| Background load | n8n may run as execution substrate if activated (D5.5 migration decision). No competing heavy inference. |
| Network | Ethernet only, stable DHCP reservation. |
| Drift indicators | Canonical memory path not accessible, brain agent not paired to bridge, LLM service not running |
| Pre-integration state | Desktop is not yet online. Baseline will be declared when Desktop is integrated in Phase 2. The n8n and legacy assets on Desktop are dormant until inventoried and classified per D5.3. Nothing on the Desktop runs until it has been reviewed. |

### 10.3 GPU3060 (Forge) Baseline

| Property | Baseline State |
|---|---|
| Power state | Always on. Cooling/fan management active. |
| On reboot | forge agent starts and confirms connection to bridge gateway. |
| Required services | OpenClaw forge agent runtime, GPU-accelerated local LLM inference service |
| Background load | GPU available for inference tasks. No GPU-consuming background processes that are not declared agent tasks. |
| Network | Ethernet only, stable DHCP reservation. |
| Drift indicators | GPU utilization pinned by undeclared process, forge agent not connected to bridge, inference latency significantly above baseline |

### 10.4 MacBook Air Baseline

| Property | Baseline State |
|---|---|
| Power state | Mobile — not required to be on. Org does not depend on it. |
| Required services | None declared for autonomous operation. Human-facing only. |
| Background load | Not relevant — not a worker node. |
| Network | Home Wi-Fi or wherever human travels. |
| Drift indicators | Not applicable — this machine is not in the autonomous operation path. |

### 10.5 Pixel / Seeker Baseline

Baseline TBD. These machines are not in the production path until Phase 5. When they are integrated, a baseline declaration is added here.

---

## 11. Agent Change Authority Within Environments

Agents operate within declared authority boundaries. What is not explicitly permitted is not permitted.

### 11.1 What Agents May Change Autonomously

Bridge (Conductor) may update the following without human approval:
- Task status fields in the Mission Control task register (in-progress, blocked, completed, escalation reason)
- Operational memory (task session logs, routing history)
- Capability register fields within the write scope defined in D1.12 §8.1 (`status`, `last_reviewed`, `review_due`, `notes`, cost usage tracking)
- Workflow library entries marked `draft` — draft creation only; activation requires human approval
- Cron schedule execution (not cron schedule definition — definition requires human approval)

Forge may update the following without human approval:
- Working memory in its own workspace
- Experiment records in D5.4 (filing results — not approving outcomes)
- Capability candidate stubs (creating `candidate` status entries in D5.7 — not approving)

Brain (Archivist) may update the following without human approval:
- Canonical memory via the promotion process (D1.7 §9)
- Memory archive entries (receiving and writing promoted entries)

### 11.2 What Agents May Not Change

No agent may change the following without human approval:
- Environment type definitions or machine-to-environment mapping (§4, §5)
- Config Layer 1 (org-wide policy) values
- Capability approval status (`approved_by`, `approval_date`, `risk_class`, scope fields)
- Autonomy level for any task class
- Escalation trigger thresholds (D1.8 §8)
- Secrets or credential entries (D1.14)
- Any D-series policy document

No agent may silently change machine-local configuration (Layer 2) without logging the change and referencing it in the next daily digest. Silent config changes are drift. (Source: D1.8 §6.5, D1.1 §3 principle 15)

### 11.3 Proposed Changes

When an agent identifies a configuration change that would require human approval, it:
1. Documents the proposed change in the task record (what, why, expected effect)
2. Surfaces it in the next daily digest as a pending proposal
3. Does not apply the change until the human approves

The agent does not delay current work to wait for approval on a non-blocking proposal. It completes what is approvable and surfaces the proposal asynchronously.

---

## 12. Reusable Methods and Workflow Consistency

This section defines how the organization converts successful ad-hoc approaches into repeatable, version-controlled, environment-consistent methods.

### 12.1 Methods and Workflows Are Consistency Controls

A workflow or method is not just a convenience — it is a consistency enforcement mechanism. When an agent follows an approved workflow, it produces the same class of output in the same way, regardless of which agent instance runs it or when. This is the precondition for reliable reuse, reliable measurement, and reliable improvement.

Environment consistency is what makes workflow reuse produce reliable results. If the same workflow runs in different environments with different dependencies, different credentials, or different tool versions, the outputs will diverge. A method that depends on a specific environment state is not a reusable method — it is a one-time procedure.

### 12.2 When a Method Should Be Registered

A method becomes a candidate for registration when:
- The same approach has been used successfully 3 or more times for the same task class
- The approach depends on specific environment state (tool versions, paths, config values) that must be consistent
- The approach produces a testable output that can be validated
- The method's removal or modification would affect multiple workflows or agents

Registration path:
- Workflow execution sequence → D5.6 (workflow draft, promoted per §5.4 of that document)
- Reusable technique or template → D5.7 as CAP-WFM (per D1.12 §10)

### 12.3 Prefer Approved Methods Over Improvisation

When an approved method or workflow exists that covers a task, it is used. Improvising a different approach for the same task class is a deviation. Deviations are flagged in the task record and surfaced in the next digest. This is not bureaucracy — it is how the organization detects when an approved method needs updating. (Source: D5.6 §4.1, D1.12 §3 principle 2)

### 12.4 Environment Consistency as a Method Requirement

When a method is registered, its environment requirements are declared:
- Which environment it runs in (Production / Sandbox / Experimental)
- Which machine it runs on
- Which tool versions or dependencies it requires
- Which config layer values it reads

If the environment changes and a previously approved method no longer works as declared, the method is flagged as inconsistent. The running environment is not silently adjusted to match the method — the method is reviewed and updated through the standard change process.

### 12.5 Sprint-Like Execution Within Approved Boundaries

The swarm executes approved work in sprint-like cycles: agents pull eligible backlog tasks, sequence them by priority, execute them, and close them without requiring human re-prompting for each step. Workflow consistency is what makes this work — a consistent environment means a consistent result means a reliable track record. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 15, D1.11 §10.3)

Agents do not need to reinvent the approach for every task. The backlog defines what to do. The workflow defines how. The environment defines the substrate. The agent executes.

---

## 13. Drift Detection and Correction Policy

### 13.1 What Counts as Drift

Drift is any departure from a declared baseline or approved configuration that was not explicitly authorized and recorded. Categories:

| Drift Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| Config drift | A Layer 2 or Layer 3 config value differs from its declared value with no update record |
| Version drift | A tool, service, or dependency is at a different version than declared in baseline |
| Service drift | A required service is not running, or an undeclared service is running |
| Capability drift | A capability is in use but its registered scope, status, or version no longer matches its actual usage |
| Method drift | An approved workflow is being executed differently from its D5.6 definition |
| Credential drift | A credential is in use that is not registered in D1.14, or is beyond its rotation window |

### 13.2 How Drift Is Detected

- Bridge performs a baseline check against declared machine state on a defined cadence (cadence TBD — registered in Mission Control cron register after Phase 1)
- Agents flag any departure from declared state they encounter during task execution
- Version checks on startup: agent validates its own environment against its declared baseline before accepting tasks
- Capability register review: bridge compares active capability usage against registered scope during daily hygiene checks

### 13.3 What May Be Auto-Corrected

Auto-correction is permitted for:
- Restarting a declared required service that has stopped (with the restart logged)
- Re-validating a baseline check that failed transiently (network timeout, etc.) — retry once before escalating
- Clearing ephemeral working memory that has exceeded its retention window (D1.7)

Auto-correction is not permitted for:
- Config value changes (even to restore a prior declared value — must be human-approved or logged)
- Reinstallation or upgrade of any tool or service
- Any change that affects environment scope, agent authority, or production state

### 13.4 Escalation for Drift

Drift that cannot be auto-corrected is:
1. Logged in the task record and the capability/method record
2. Surfaced in the next daily digest for non-critical drift
3. Escalated immediately (D1.9 §10.3 format) for drift that is affecting live production tasks

Bridge does not attempt to "fix" unknown drift by self-directed environment reconstruction. It reports the drift and halts affected tasks until the human provides direction. (Source: D1.8 §8 escalation trigger 1 — scope boundary)

---

## 14. Recovery and Rebuild Expectations

### 14.1 The Core Standard

Any production environment must be rebuildable from version-controlled documentation and approved configuration. If a machine is lost or fully corrupted, the organization must be able to reconstruct its environment to a known-good baseline by following recorded documentation — not by trying to remember what was done.

This standard is not aspirational. It is the minimum requirement for any production environment.

### 14.2 What Rebuild Requires

For a rebuild to be possible:
- All configuration decisions must be committed to version control before they are applied (§8.4)
- All installed tools and services must have their setup path documented in D2.x runbooks
- All secrets must be registered in D1.14 with enough information to re-generate or retrieve them
- All baseline state declarations (§10) must be current and committed
- All approved capabilities must have their activation requirements recorded in D5.7

A machine rebuilt from these sources should produce an environment that is functionally identical to the one it replaced — not a best-effort approximation.

### 14.3 No Critical Dependency on Machine State Alone

No critical operational behavior may depend on state that lives only in a running machine's memory, an untracked local file, or a chat history. This includes:
- Workflow methods known only to a single agent's context
- API endpoint configurations not committed to version control
- Routing decisions not documented in D1.9
- Capability activations not registered in D5.7

### 14.4 Recovery Restores Declared Baseline, Not Improvised State

When a machine is recovered, the goal is to restore its declared baseline (§10) — not to reconstruct what it happened to be running at the moment of failure. If the declared baseline is insufficient for current needs, the baseline is updated through the change process before the rebuild. Rebuilding to a different state than the declared baseline without updating the declaration is a form of configuration drift. (Source: D1.2 guardrail 5)

---

## 15. Blocked / TBD Environment Decisions

| Item | Blocked On | Expected Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Desktop OS and actual config path layout | Desktop not yet integrated — OS TBD (likely Windows or Linux) | Phase 2 integration; update §10.2 and D1.2 §14 |
| Desktop canonical memory path | Desktop not online; memory store technology TBD (D1.7) | Phase 2 after Desktop is integrated and memory store is selected |
| GPU3060 OS, inference stack, and actual config paths | GPU3060 not yet integrated — OS TBD | Phase 2 integration; update §10.3 and D1.2 §14 |
| Sandbox partitioning on Desktop | Desktop not online; sandbox path isolation strategy depends on OS and filesystem | Phase 2 after Desktop OS is known |
| Remote git mirror for Mission Control | No decision made on whether Mac Mini's git repo should have a remote — local-only creates single point of failure risk | Phase 1 setup decision; options: GitHub/private, NAS, or secondary machine mirror |
| Edge environment role (Pixel / Seeker) | Phase 5 design not yet started; device capabilities not pinned | Phase 5 design; update §5 and §10.5 |
| Baseline check cadence and automation | Cron infrastructure not yet running; bridge not yet operational | Phase 1 after bridge is online; register in Mission Control cron register |
| LAN-exposed OpenClaw gateway policy | D1.9 §13 defers decision; depends on whether Phase 2 inter-agent routing requires LAN binding | Phase 1/2 decision; record in D1.9 when decided |
| MacBook Air Mission Control sync method | Currently human manually syncs; no declared sync mechanism | Phase 1 decision; record in D2.1 once bridge is operational |

---

## 16. Immediate Environment Priorities

These are the concrete, sequenced actions required before environment control can be considered operational.

### Before Phase 1 Exits

1. **Initialize git repository in Mission Control workspace** — `git init` in `~/Cursor Projects/mission-control/`. Create an initial commit with all existing D-series documents. Establish `.gitignore` that explicitly excludes secrets, credentials, and any path that D1.14 will designate as the secrets store. This must exist before any agent writes to Mission Control. (Source: §8.1)

2. **Write D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md** — Environment control is incomplete without a defined secrets management policy. D1.14 must exist before any credential is registered, any bot token is configured, or any API key is set. D1.14 is the next document in the sequence. (Source: §6, §8.3, §10)

3. **Declare Mac Mini baseline in committed form** — Write the Mac Mini baseline declaration as a committed file in Mission Control (not just as prose in this document). The baseline file must be referenced by bridge as the authoritative check target. This becomes the first drift detection anchor. (Source: §10.1)

4. **Decide on Mission Control git remote** — Before Phase 1 operation begins, decide: does the Mac Mini git repo have a remote backup? Options are a private GitHub repo, a local NAS mirror, or mirroring to another basement machine once Phase 2 is live. Document the decision. A single-point git repo is an organizational risk. (Source: §15, D1.2 §13)

5. **Confirm bridge write access scope is implemented** — Before bridge operates on any registry, verify that its write authority (D1.12 §8.1, §11.1 of this document) is implemented as config-level permissions, not just as documented policy. Trust but verify. (Source: §11.1)

### Phase 2 Setup (When Cluster Comes Online)

6. **Survey Desktop before touching it** — Before any configuration change on the Desktop, inventory its existing state: OS, running services, n8n status, local ports, active legacy processes. File results in D5.3. Nothing runs until classified. (Source: D1.1 §2, D1.2 §3, §10.2)

7. **Establish sandbox isolation path on Desktop** — Once Desktop OS is known, define the specific directory path and access controls that constitute the sandbox environment on Desktop. Commit the declaration before any sandbox work begins. (Source: §4.2, §5)

8. **Commit GPU3060 and Desktop baseline declarations** — After Phase 2 integration, write and commit baseline declarations for both machines (§10.2, §10.3). Bridge's drift detection cannot cover these machines until their baselines exist. (Source: §13.2)

---

_This document defines how environments are controlled, not how they are set up. Setup steps are in D2.x runbooks. Secrets are in D1.14. The document sequence continues: the immediate next step is D1.14-SECRETS_POLICY.md — no bot, no API key, no credential may be activated before that policy exists._

docs.title: D1.14 — SECRETS_POLICY
docs.content:
# D1.14 — SECRETS_POLICY
**Autonomous AI Organization — Credential Classification, Storage, Activation, and Governance**
_Last updated: 2026-03-13 | Status: Active | Parent: D1.1-ROADMAP.md_

---

## 1. Purpose

This document governs how secrets are managed across the organization. A secret is any piece of access material that, if exposed, would allow unauthorized actors to act as the organization: API keys, bot tokens, platform credentials, service account passwords, signing keys, and webhook secrets.

Secrets are not configuration. They are not convenience. They are the keys to the organization's external presence, financial exposure, and communication channels. Their governance requires the same discipline as financial controls — because in this organization, they effectively are financial and operational controls.

**This document is a hard gate.** Nothing requiring a credential may be activated until that credential's governance is defined here. This includes:
- The Telegram bot token (primary coordination channel)
- The Anthropic API key (primary inference)
- Any Discord bot token (optional — not activated by default)
- Any xAI / Grok key (inactive until D1.10 gate is satisfied)
- Any platform, commerce, or service credential

The fact that a key has been obtained does not mean it is ready to use. A secret is not active until it is registered, scoped, stored in an approved location, and the capability it unlocks has been approved per D1.12.

**This document governs:**
- What constitutes a secret and how secrets are classified
- Which environments each secret class is permitted in
- Who has authority over secret creation, scoping, rotation, and revocation
- How secrets are registered (metadata only — values never appear in documents)
- Where secrets may be stored and where they are explicitly forbidden
- How agents may and may not interact with secrets at runtime
- What counts as compromise and what the response is
- What is logged and what is never logged
- Which secrets are activated at each phase

**What this document does not cover:**
- Setup steps for specific secret stores — D2.x runbooks
- Environment boundaries (the host environments these secrets operate in) — D1.13-ENV_CONTROL.md
- Capability acquisition (the capabilities these secrets unlock) — D1.12-CAPABILITY_ACQUISITION_POLICY.md
- Communication channel configuration — D1.11-COMMUNICATION_MODES.md, D1.9-BROKER_POLICY.md

---

## 2. Scope

This policy covers every piece of access material that the organization holds or uses, regardless of source, format, or the machine it lives on.

| What Is Covered | Examples |
|---|---|
| API keys | Anthropic key, xAI key, OpenClaw provider keys |
| Bot tokens | Telegram bot token, Discord bot token |
| Platform auth tokens | Commerce platform tokens, social channel auth, OAuth tokens |
| Service account credentials | Third-party service logins, SaaS accounts used by agents |
| Webhook secrets / shared secrets | Inbound webhook validation tokens, HMAC signing keys |
| Local signing or encryption material | GPG keys, SSH private keys for inter-machine access |
| Machine-local access credentials | SSH authorized keys, screen sharing access, local admin credentials |

All five machines are in scope. All phases are in scope — including Phase 0 (the only "secrets" that exist now are machine access credentials for the Mac Mini).

---

## 3. Secrets Policy Principles

1. **Least privilege, always.** Every secret is scoped to the minimum access needed for the specific capability it unlocks. Broader scope than necessary is not a convenience — it is an unacceptable risk surface. The default scope for any new credential is the narrowest possible.

2. **One authoritative registration record per secret.** Every secret in active use has exactly one registration entry in the secret registry (§7). Secrets that exist but are not registered are non-compliant. Secrets that have multiple registration entries are ambiguous and must be resolved immediately.

3. **Secret values never enter Mission Control git.** The secret registry tracks metadata — ID, type, scope, owner, expiry, status. The actual credential value is never written to a D-series document, a task record, a registry entry, a proof artifact, or any file in the Mission Control workspace. A value committed to git, even briefly, is treated as compromised. (Source: D1.13 §8.3)

4. **Process memory is temporary; files are governed.** Loading a secret into process memory for a single operation is acceptable and expected. Writing a secret to any file — even a temp file — requires that file to be in an approved storage location. A secret value written to a log, a debug output, or a cache file is an exposure incident.

5. **Production secrets do not enter sandbox or experimental environments.** This is not a preference — it is a hard rule. If a capability cannot be tested without production credentials, either the test is incorrectly scoped or a test credential must be provisioned. Reusing a production API key in an experiment to "save time" is a policy violation. (Source: D1.13 §4, §5)

6. **Human approves access scope.** The human decides which agent, on which machine, in which environment, may use a given secret. Agents propose. Humans approve. No agent may self-authorize broader credential access, even if it believes the task requires it. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 15, D1.4 §3)

7. **Expired or suspected-leaked secrets are treated as compromised.** "It probably wasn't actually leaked" is not a valid operating assumption. If a secret was exposed — even briefly, even in a context that seems internal-only — it is rotated before it is used again. (Source: §11)

8. **Self-managed execution does not imply self-managed credentials.** The swarm manages its own task execution within approved boundaries. Credential scope, rotation, and activation are not execution responsibilities — they are governance responsibilities. They remain with the human regardless of how autonomous the swarm becomes. (Source: D1.1 §3 principle 15)

9. **A secret is not active until its governance is complete.** Obtaining an API key is not the same as activating it. Activation requires: registration, scope approval, storage in an approved location, and approval of the capability it unlocks (D1.12 §9.1). The key sitting in a notes file is not an active capability — it is an unregistered risk.

10. **Credential governance is a prerequisite for reusable workflows.** An approved workflow that depends on a credential cannot be executed reliably if that credential's governance is incomplete. Workflows do not carry credential values — they reference registered credential IDs. The credential management layer resolves the ID to the value at runtime, within scope. (Source: D1.13 §12)

---

## 4. Secret Classes

### 4.1 API Keys
